title: The case for keeping a diary no one will read
summary: Argue why a private daily log — dull, repetitive, unshared — compounds over years into something more honest than memory, even when each single entry feels worthless.
topic: Why a deliberately private, unremarkable daily journal pays off only across years, not days
prompt: Write a 450–600-word argumentative essay defending the habit of keeping a private diary that no one else will ever read, even though any single entry seems trivial. Argue the paradox that the value is invisible day to day and only emerges once the entries accumulate. Give two concrete examples and concede one fair objection. Use at least one cleft sentence, one passive construction, and hedging language.
hintStarters: On any given evening, the entry I write seems almost too dull to bother with, and yet…
What a diary preserves is not the dramatic day but the ordinary one, the kind that…
Granted, a journal can curdle into mere brooding, a place where the same complaint is…
It is only when you read a stranger's handwriting and realise it is your own that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một bài ghi / một mục nhật ký
tích lũy dần và nhân lên
một bản ghi quá trình / thành tích
đắm chìm trong suy nghĩ tiêu cực

sampleText:
On any given evening, the entry I write seems almost too dull to bother with: I slept badly, the meeting ran long, the soup was good. Nothing happened that I would tell a friend, let alone a reader. If a diary had to justify itself one page at a time, it would lose the argument every night. That is exactly why people abandon the habit by February, and exactly why they are wrong to.

The value of a private journal is not found in any single entry; it is released only when the entries are read back together, months or years later. What a diary preserves is not the dramatic day but the ordinary one — the kind memory throws away first, because nothing about it asked to be remembered. A holiday or a funeral will lodge itself in the mind without help. What vanishes is the texture of a normal Tuesday: who you were annoyed with, what you were afraid of, the small ambition you have since forgotten you ever had. Those Tuesdays are where a life mostly happens, and they are precisely what no other record keeps.

Consider two readers of their own past. The first kept nothing, and now reconstructs her twenties from a handful of photographs and a story she has told so often it has hardened into myth. The second kept a line or two each night and, rereading them, is startled to find she was happier than she remembers, or angrier, or simply mistaken about when things began. The diary does not flatter her. It refuses to be retouched. A memory will quietly edit itself to suit the person you have become; an old entry will not, which is why it can still embarrass you. It is precisely that refusal to be revised that makes the cumulative record worth more than the polished story.

There is also the matter of audience, or rather its absence. The moment a journal is written for someone else, the writing begins to perform. Sentences get tidied, motives get improved, the cruel thought is left out. A diary that no one will ever read is the one place where you are not auditioning, and so it is the one place where the unflattering pattern — the same fear repeated, the same person blamed — can finally be seen. Patterns are invisible at a single point. They appear only across the accumulated days, and only to someone honest enough not to look away.

Granted, a journal can curdle into mere brooding, a place where the same complaint is rehearsed until it deepens. That objection is fair. A diary kept badly becomes a museum of grievances, and there are weeks when not writing would have been the healthier choice. The remedy, though, is not to abandon the habit but to keep it honestly — to record what changed, not only what hurt.

It is only when you read a stranger's handwriting and realise it is your own that the point of all those negligible nights becomes clear. The habit asks almost nothing on any single evening, which is why it is so easy to quit and so quietly powerful when it is not. You are not writing for tonight. You are leaving evidence for a future self who will otherwise have nothing but a story, and stories, unlike entries, are always told by the winner.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences to relocate the value
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a diary preserves is not the dramatic day but the ordinary one' (what-cleft) và 'It is precisely that refusal to be revised that makes the cumulative record worth more …' (it-cleft) dùng câu chẻ để dời trọng tâm sang điều thường bị bỏ qua, làm nổi bật luận điểm chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a diary preserves is not the dramatic day but the ordinary one' (what-cleft) and 'It is precisely that refusal to be revised that makes the cumulative record worth more …' (it-cleft) use clefts to shift the focus onto what is usually overlooked, foregrounding the thesis.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to depersonalise the slow payoff
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'it is released only when the entries are read back together' và 'It refuses to be retouched' dùng bị động để biến giá trị của nhật ký thành điều xuất hiện theo thời gian chứ không do một hành động đơn lẻ, phản ánh bản chất tích lũy của thói quen.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'it is released only when the entries are read back together' and 'It refuses to be retouched' use the passive to present the diary's value as something that emerges over time rather than from a single act, mirroring the habit's cumulative nature.

sampleTranslationVi:
Vào bất kỳ buổi tối nào, bài ghi tôi viết có vẻ quá tẻ nhạt để bận tâm: tôi ngủ không ngon, cuộc họp kéo dài, món súp ngon. Chẳng có gì xảy ra đến mức tôi muốn kể cho một người bạn, chứ đừng nói đến một người đọc. Nếu một cuốn nhật ký phải tự biện minh từng trang một, nó sẽ thua cuộc tranh luận mỗi đêm. Đó chính là lý do người ta từ bỏ thói quen này khi đến tháng Hai, và cũng chính là lý do họ sai khi làm vậy.

Giá trị của một cuốn nhật ký riêng tư không nằm ở bất kỳ bài ghi đơn lẻ nào; nó chỉ được giải phóng khi các bài ghi được đọc lại cùng nhau, nhiều tháng hay nhiều năm sau. Cái mà một cuốn nhật ký lưu giữ không phải là ngày kịch tính mà là ngày bình thường — loại mà trí nhớ vứt bỏ trước tiên, bởi chẳng có gì trong nó đòi được nhớ. Một kỳ nghỉ hay một đám tang sẽ tự khắc ghi vào tâm trí mà không cần trợ giúp. Cái biến mất là kết cấu của một ngày thứ Ba bình thường: bạn bực mình với ai, bạn sợ điều gì, tham vọng nhỏ mà từ đó bạn đã quên rằng mình từng có. Những ngày thứ Ba ấy là nơi một cuộc đời chủ yếu diễn ra, và chúng chính là thứ không một bản ghi nào khác giữ lại.

Hãy xét đến hai người đọc về quá khứ của chính mình. Người thứ nhất chẳng giữ gì, và giờ dựng lại tuổi đôi mươi của mình từ một nhúm ảnh và một câu chuyện cô kể nhiều đến mức nó đã hóa cứng thành huyền thoại. Người thứ hai mỗi đêm giữ lại một hai dòng và, khi đọc lại, kinh ngạc nhận ra mình từng hạnh phúc hơn mình nhớ, hoặc tức giận hơn, hoặc đơn giản là nhầm về thời điểm mọi chuyện bắt đầu. Cuốn nhật ký không tâng bốc cô. Nó từ chối bị chỉnh sửa. Một ký ức sẽ lặng lẽ tự biên tập để phù hợp với con người bạn đã trở thành; một bài ghi cũ thì không, và đó là lý do nó vẫn có thể khiến bạn ngượng. Chính sự từ chối bị sửa lại đó khiến bản ghi tích lũy có giá trị hơn câu chuyện đã gọt giũa.

Còn có vấn đề về người đọc, hay đúng hơn là sự vắng mặt của họ. Khoảnh khắc một cuốn nhật ký được viết cho người khác, việc viết bắt đầu trình diễn. Câu chữ được dọn dẹp, động cơ được tô hồng, ý nghĩ tàn nhẫn bị bỏ ra ngoài. Một cuốn nhật ký mà không ai sẽ đọc là nơi duy nhất bạn không đang thử vai, và vì thế là nơi duy nhất mà khuôn mẫu kém đẹp đẽ — cùng nỗi sợ lặp lại, cùng một người bị đổ lỗi — cuối cùng có thể được nhìn thấy. Các khuôn mẫu là vô hình tại một điểm đơn lẻ. Chúng chỉ xuất hiện qua những ngày tích lũy, và chỉ với người trung thực đủ để không ngoảnh đi.

Công nhận, một cuốn nhật ký có thể hóa chua thành chỉ còn sự đắm chìm tiêu cực, một nơi mà cùng một lời than vãn được diễn lại cho đến khi nó càng sâu thêm. Phản đối đó là công bằng. Một cuốn nhật ký được giữ tệ sẽ trở thành một bảo tàng của những nỗi bất bình, và có những tuần mà không viết lẽ ra là lựa chọn lành mạnh hơn. Tuy nhiên, cách chữa không phải là bỏ thói quen mà là giữ nó một cách trung thực — ghi lại điều gì đã thay đổi, chứ không chỉ điều gì đã làm đau.

Chỉ khi bạn đọc nét chữ của một người lạ và nhận ra đó là của chính mình thì điểm mấu chốt của tất cả những đêm không đáng kể ấy mới trở nên rõ ràng. Thói quen này hầu như không đòi hỏi gì trong bất kỳ buổi tối đơn lẻ nào, đó là lý do nó dễ bỏ đến vậy và lại mạnh mẽ một cách lặng lẽ đến thế khi nó không bị bỏ. Bạn không viết cho đêm nay. Bạn đang để lại bằng chứng cho một bản thân tương lai mà nếu không thì sẽ chẳng có gì ngoài một câu chuyện, và những câu chuyện, không như các bài ghi, luôn được kể bởi người chiến thắng.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to relocate the diary's value onto the ordinary day?
options: What a diary preserves is not the dramatic day but the ordinary one.
A diary preserves what the ordinary day is not the dramatic one.
The ordinary day is what a diary not the dramatic preserves one.
Preserves a diary what is the ordinary not dramatic day one.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What a diary preserves is not X but Y' is a what-cleft that foregrounds the ordinary day.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What a diary preserves is …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to name what makes the record valuable?
options: The cumulative record refuses to be revised which is worth more than the story.
It is precisely that refusal to be revised that makes the cumulative record worth more than the polished story.
Worth more is the cumulative record it that refusal revised to be precisely.
That refusal precisely is what the record makes revised worth the story polished.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X that …' is an it-cleft spotlighting the refusal to be revised as the source of value.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to present the value as something that emerges over time?
options: You release the value only when you read the entries back.
It is released only when the entries are read back together.
Releasing the value is what reading the entries back together does.
Read back the entries together it the value is releasing only when.
mcQuestions.explanation: Two passives ('is released', 'are read back') frame the payoff as something that happens, not an act by a named doer.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is + past participle' twice?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to compound' mean as the essay uses it?
options: To mix two substances together
To build up gradually so the total grows larger than the parts suggest
To make a problem suddenly worse
To write something down quickly
mcQuestions.explanation: Here 'compound' describes small entries accumulating into something greater over time.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of small amounts adding up across years.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option concedes the objection most fairly and in keeping with the essay's tone?
options: Only weak people waste time writing about their feelings every night.
Granted, a journal can curdle into mere brooding, a place where the same complaint is rehearsed until it deepens.
Obviously diaries are pointless and everyone who keeps one is deluded.
Of course no real benefit could ever come from such a boring habit.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' fairly admits the risk of brooding without abandoning the argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits a genuine downside without sneering?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges appropriately rather than overstating?
options: A memory always lies and can never be trusted about anything at all.
A memory will quietly edit itself to suit the person you have become.
A memory never changes once it is formed and is perfectly accurate forever.
Edit itself a memory the person become you have to suit quietly will.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Will quietly edit itself to suit' describes a tendency without the absolutes 'always lies' or 'never changes'.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid both 'always' and 'never'; pick the measured claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why does writing for an audience weaken a diary?
options: Because the writing begins to perform, tidying motives and leaving out cruel thoughts
Because other people steal the ideas in it
Because handwriting becomes illegible to readers
Because the diary then has to be published
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that an audience makes the writing 'perform', so honesty is lost.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the paragraph about audience and auditioning.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps the essay's analytical register?
options: Honestly, journaling is just a vibe and you either get it or you don't, lol.
Patterns are invisible at a single point; they appear only across the accumulated days.
Basically diaries are kinda deep if you're into that whole self-help thing.
It's super sad how people just forget everything, you know what I mean?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the claim precisely; the others slip into casual filler.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best moves from the concession back to the main argument?
options: In conclusion, diaries are simply a waste of time.
The remedy, though, is not to abandon the habit but to keep it honestly — to record what changed, not only what hurt.
Anyway, none of this really matters in the end.
On the contrary, no one has ever benefited from writing anything down.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The remedy, though, …' accepts the risk just conceded and steers back to the thesis.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase keeps the concession but reasserts the position?

options: While something is still happening
Looking back on something after it has happened
Hiding something from view
Predicting the future accurately
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In hindsight' means with the understanding gained after the event.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of judging an event only once it is over.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most cleanly?
options: who you were annoyed with, what you were afraid of, the small ambition you have since forgotten
who annoyed you and being afraid and to forget the small ambition since
the one you annoyed with were, afraid being of what, forgetting ambition small
annoyed who with, afraid of what were you, ambition the small forgotten you
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched relative clauses form a balanced list; the others scramble the structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three items in the same grammatical shape?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay finally claim a diary leaves for the future self?
options: A polished story that flatters the writer
Evidence that resists the self-serving version a memory would invent
Proof that the writer was always right
A record that should be shown to others
mcQuestions.explanation: The ending contrasts honest 'evidence' with the winner's edited 'story'.

options: To examine something in great detail
To deal with or mention something quickly to hide its faults
To make something shiny
To translate something into another language
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Gloss over' means to pass over a problem lightly so it seems less serious.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of skating quickly past an uncomfortable detail.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's central paradox?
options: Every single diary entry is dramatic and exciting on its own.
The habit asks almost nothing on any single evening, yet grows quietly powerful only once the entries accumulate.
Diaries are valuable each night but worthless when read together.
A diary should be written for an audience to have any worth.
mcQuestions.explanation: The paradox is that nightly value is negligible while the cumulative value is large.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option contrasting one night with the accumulated whole.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the em-dash insertion correctly to add an aside?
options: What a diary preserves is not the dramatic day but the ordinary one — the kind memory throws away first.
What a diary — preserves is not the dramatic day but — the ordinary kind memory one.
The ordinary one — what a diary preserves not — the dramatic day is the kind.
Preserves a diary what — is the ordinary day — not dramatic memory throws first.
mcQuestions.explanation: The dash introduces an explanatory aside; remove it and the main clause still stands.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets you delete the dashed part and keep a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'negligible' mean as used here?
options: Careless or irresponsible
So small as to be hardly worth considering
Impossible to forget
Extremely valuable
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Negligible' describes the tiny nightly effort that seems not worth bothering with.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of an amount too small to matter on its own.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a record you stopped keeping — a journal, a budget, a training log. What did you tell yourself each day to justify skipping it, and would that excuse survive being read back a year later?
